annotations.label: Linear regression

anchor.snippet: linear regression is a model that estimates the relationship between a scalar response

annotations.note: Linear regression as a statistical model is not defined anywhere in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear in parameters

anchor.snippet: linear in its unknown parameters

annotations.note: The regression notion of linearity in parameters is not formalized (only abstract linear/affine maps exist, unrelated to this statistical sense).

annotations.label: Examples of linear regression

anchor.snippet: In the above equations, the coefficients (parameters) are linear

annotations.note: No regression model examples appear in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Examples of non-linear regression

anchor.snippet: In the above equations, the coefficients (parameters) are non-linear.

annotations.note: No regression model examples appear in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple vs multiple linear regression

anchor.snippet: A model with exactly one explanatory variable is a simple linear regression

annotations.note: Neither simple nor multiple linear regression is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Conditional mean is affine

anchor.snippet: the conditional mean of the response given the values of the explanatory variables (or predictors) is assumed to be an affine function of those values

annotations.note: The core modeling assumption that E[Y|X] is affine in X is not formalized as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear regression model

anchor.snippet: a linear regression model assumes that the relationship between the dependent variable y and the vector of regressors x is linear

annotations.note: The linear regression model y = Xβ + ε is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regressand / dependent variable

anchor.snippet: of the variable called the regressand

annotations.note: Regression terminology such as regressand is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regressors / design matrix

anchor.snippet: which are known as regressors , exogenous variables

annotations.note: The notion of a design matrix of regressors is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parameter vector / regression coefficients

anchor.snippet: Its elements are known as effects or regression coefficients

annotations.note: Regression coefficients are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Error term

anchor.snippet: This part of the model is called the error term , disturbance term

annotations.note: The regression error/disturbance term is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sum of squared errors

anchor.snippet: it is common to use the sum of squared errors

annotations.note: The regression sum-of-squared-errors objective is not formalized (only the generic squared Euclidean norm exists, not this loss).

annotations.label: Ball tossed in the air

anchor.snippet: Consider a situation where a small ball is being tossed up in the air

annotations.note: This illustrative physics regression example is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Unique effect (interpretation of coefficient)

anchor.snippet: the interpretation of β j is the expected change in y for a one-unit change in x j when the other covariates are held fixed

annotations.note: The interpretive notion of a coefficient's unique effect is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Marginal effect

anchor.snippet: the marginal effect of x j on y can be assessed using a correlation coefficient or simple linear regression model relating only x j to y

annotations.note: The regression marginal-effect interpretation (total derivative of y w.r.t. x_j) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple linear regression

anchor.snippet: The simplest case of a single scalar predictor variable x and a single scalar response variable y is known as simple linear regression

annotations.note: Simple linear regression is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiple linear regression

anchor.snippet: Multiple linear regression is a generalization of simple linear regression to the case of more than one independent variable

annotations.note: Multiple linear regression is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multivariate linear regression

anchor.snippet: In the more general multivariate linear regression, there is one equation of the above form for each of m > 1 dependent variables

annotations.note: Multivariate linear regression is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: General linear model

anchor.snippet: The general linear model considers the situation when the response variable is not a scalar

annotations.note: The statistical general linear model is not in Mathlib (the 'general linear group' GL is unrelated).

annotations.label: Heteroscedasticity

anchor.snippet: i.e. the errors for different response variables may have different variances

annotations.note: The notion of heteroscedasticity (non-constant error variance) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weighted least squares (heteroscedastic)

anchor.snippet: weighted least squares is a method for estimating linear regression models when the response variables may have different error variances

annotations.note: Weighted least squares estimation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalized linear model

anchor.snippet: The Generalized linear model (GLM) is a framework for modeling response variables that are bounded or discrete.

annotations.note: Generalized linear models are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Link function

anchor.snippet: Generalized linear models allow for an arbitrary link function

annotations.note: The GLM link function concept is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hierarchical linear models

anchor.snippet: organizes the data into a hierarchy of regressions

annotations.note: Hierarchical/multilevel linear models are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Errors-in-variables models

anchor.snippet: Errors-in-variables models (or "measurement error models") extend the traditional linear regression model to allow the predictor variables X to be observed with error.

annotations.note: Errors-in-variables (measurement error) models are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Group effect

anchor.snippet: is defined as a linear combination of their parameters

annotations.note: The regression group-effect definition is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Meaningful group effect

anchor.snippet: is said to be meaningful if the underlying simultaneous changes of the

annotations.note: The notion of a meaningful group effect is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Minimum-variance unbiased estimator of group effect

anchor.snippet: and its minimum-variance unbiased linear estimator is

annotations.note: Mathlib has general decision-theoretic estimators/risk infrastructure but no unbiasedness or MVUE-of-group-effect result.

annotations.label: Dempster–Shafer swept-matrix representation

anchor.snippet: a linear regression model may be represented as a partially swept matrix

annotations.note: The swept-matrix (Dempster–Shafer) representation of regression is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Least-squares optimum parameter

anchor.snippet: the optimum parameter vector is defined as such that minimizes the sum of mean squared loss

annotations.note: The least-squares minimizer is not defined as such, but the underlying fact that the orthogonal projection attains the minimal distance is formalized.

annotations.label: Convex loss minimized at gradient zero

anchor.snippet: As the loss function is convex , the optimum solution lies at gradient zero.

annotations.note: Mathlib proves a local minimum of a convex function is a global minimum (with Fermat's theorem giving gradient zero), supplying the general principle but not this regression-specific statement.

annotations.label: Normal equations (gradient zero solution)

anchor.snippet: Setting the gradient to zero produces the optimum parameter

annotations.note: The regression normal equations are not stated, but the geometric least-squares characterization via orthogonal projection (residual orthogonal to the subspace) is formalized.

annotations.label: Gauss–Markov theorem

anchor.snippet: This is provided by the Gauss–Markov theorem .

annotations.note: The Gauss–Markov theorem (OLS is BLUE) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximum likelihood estimation

anchor.snippet: Maximum likelihood estimation can be performed when the distribution of the error terms is known to belong to a certain parametric family

annotations.note: Maximum likelihood estimation is not defined in Mathlib (only log-likelihood-ratio/KL-divergence infrastructure exists, not MLE).

annotations.label: Normal errors give OLS estimate

anchor.snippet: the resulting estimate is identical to the OLS estimate

annotations.note: The result that MLE under Gaussian errors equals the OLS estimate is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Least squares equals MLE

anchor.snippet: the result of the least squares method is the same as the result of the maximum likelihood estimation method

annotations.note: The equivalence of least squares and maximum likelihood under normal errors is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regularized (ridge/lasso) regression

anchor.snippet: deliberately introduce bias into the estimation of β in order to reduce the variability of the estimate

annotations.note: Ridge/lasso regularized regression is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Least absolute deviation regression

anchor.snippet: Least absolute deviation (LAD) regression is a robust estimation technique

annotations.note: Least absolute deviation regression is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LAD equals MLE under Laplace model

anchor.snippet: It is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation under a Laplace distribution model

annotations.note: The equivalence of LAD and MLE under a Laplace model is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adaptive estimation optimal estimator

anchor.snippet: then the optimal estimator is the 2-step MLE

annotations.note: Adaptive estimation and the 2-step MLE optimality result are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bayesian linear regression

anchor.snippet: Bayesian linear regression applies the framework of Bayesian statistics to linear regression.

annotations.note: Bayesian linear regression is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantile regression

anchor.snippet: Quantile regression focuses on the conditional quantiles of y given X

annotations.note: Quantile regression is not formalized in Mathlib (no quantile machinery found).

annotations.label: Mixed models

anchor.snippet: Mixed models are widely used to analyze linear regression relationships involving dependent data

annotations.note: Mixed (random-effects) models are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Principal component regression

anchor.snippet: is used when the number of predictor variables is large, or when strong correlations exist among the predictor variables

annotations.note: Principal component regression is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Least-angle regression

anchor.snippet: is an estimation procedure for linear regression models that was developed to handle high-dimensional covariate vectors

annotations.note: Least-angle regression is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Theil–Sen estimator

anchor.snippet: The Theil–Sen estimator is a simple robust estimation technique that chooses the slope of the fit line to be the median of the slopes

annotations.note: The Theil–Sen estimator is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trend line

anchor.snippet: A trend line represents a trend, the long-term movement in time series data

annotations.note: The time-series trend line concept is not formalized in Mathlib.
